summary: Paul warns of coming discipline, calls believers to self-examination and restoration, and closes with a blessing of grace, love, and fellowship.

outline.title: A Final Warning Before the Third Visit
outline.summary: Paul warns that persistent sin will be addressed by the power of the risen Christ when he comes again.

outline.title: Examine Yourselves and Be Restored
outline.summary: The Corinthians must test themselves, pursue maturity, and receive Paul’s authority as a means of building up.

outline.title: Final Appeal and Trinitarian Blessing
outline.summary: Paul closes with a call to unity and peace and blesses the church with Christ’s grace, God’s love, and the Spirit’s fellowship.

verses.bibleText: This is the third time I am coming to you. In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul announces that the visit now before him will be his third. Earlier he had spoken of being ready to come a third time; readiness or intention is not itself another completed visit. As he writes here, the third coming still lies ahead. The natural sequence is an initial visit in which the congregation was established, a later painful visit, and the visit he is now preparing to make. His words carry the weight of an accumulated relationship: the Corinthians have not received one hurried warning from a distant stranger. They have known his ministry, his sorrow, his patience, and his repeated appeals.

The statement about “two or three witnesses” brings the anticipated visit under the rule of careful judgment. In its earlier biblical setting, this principle protected people from condemnation resting on a single, possibly biased accusation. A serious charge was not to be settled by rumor, personal hostility, prejudice, or an impulsive verdict. Truth had to be responsibly established. Paul is therefore not planning to arrive in anger and punish whomever his opponents name. He will deal with actual matters on a sound basis.

The connection between the witnesses and Paul’s repeated visits or warnings is not explained in detail, so it is wise not to force the language beyond what it says. The main emphasis is clear: his coming will bring matters into the light and require an honest reckoning. Patience does not eliminate accountability, and accountability must not abandon fairness.

This principle must never become a technical excuse for dismissing credible harm or silencing someone who suffered where no audience was present. The command does not teach leaders to protect wrongdoing until several eyewitnesses appear. It calls them to seek truth diligently, weigh testimony responsibly, guard against false accusation, and refuse careless condemnation. Both the accused and the injured are served when judgment is neither gullible nor prejudiced.

Paul is preparing the church for a serious visit, but not an arbitrary one. The same apostle who insists that persistent sin must be faced also insists that charges must be established. Holiness and justice belong together.

verses.bibleText: I told you before, and foretell you, as if I were present, the second time; and being absent now I write to them which heretofore have sinned, and to all other, that, if I come again, I will not spare:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The warning becomes more direct. Paul reminds the Corinthians that, during his earlier presence, he had already spoken beforehand, and now, while absent, he warns them again. His coming response will therefore not be a sudden outburst. Those who had sinned previously have been given time, instruction, and opportunity to repent. The letter itself is another act of patience, placing the seriousness of the situation before them before he arrives.

“I will not spare” does not describe violence, revenge, public cruelty, or the satisfaction of humiliating people who have disappointed him. It means that he will no longer allow persistent, unrepentant sin to remain unaddressed. He will use the authority entrusted to him to confront what is corrupting the congregation. His firmness may involve painful correction, but it is moral and pastoral rather than vindictive. He is not defending a wounded ego; he is refusing to treat rebellion as harmless.

The warning reaches both those who had sinned before and “all the rest.” No favored group is placed beyond accountability. Earlier offenders cannot assume that the passing of time has erased the need for repentance, and others cannot imagine that Paul’s attention to prominent cases leaves their own conduct untouched. Grace is not indifference. God’s patience gives room to turn; it does not rename evil as good.

Paul’s restraint had apparently been interpreted by some as weakness. They may have assumed that because he delayed another painful encounter, wrote with tears, and appealed rather than immediately acting, he lacked the courage or authority to deal with the matter. The coming visit will show that gentleness is not helplessness. Christian patience is strong enough to wait, plead, and forgive, yet holy enough to act when continued delay would protect sin rather than people.

verses.bibleText: Since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you-ward is not weak, but is mighty in you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Behind the warning lies the Corinthians’ demand for proof that Christ is speaking through Paul. They have placed the apostle on trial and appear to want a kind of evidence that matches their preferred picture of power. Throughout the conflict, outward impressiveness has competed with the pattern of the cross. A commanding presence, polished speech, visible success, forceful self-assertion, or a dramatic display of authority could easily seem more convincing than patient suffering and self-giving love.

Paul does not deny that Christ’s authority must be real. He denies that worldly display is the proper measure of it. The Christ who speaks through him “is not weak” toward the Corinthians but powerful among them. Christ’s power has already been active wherever the gospel has exposed hearts, created faith, called people from sin, and sustained His church. That power may also be shown through decisive correction if the congregation continues to resist the truth. Yet it must not be imagined as intimidation or domination. Christ does not need to imitate proud human rulers in order to prove that He is Lord.

The demand for proof is therefore deeply ironic. The Corinthians themselves exist as a congregation because Christ worked through the ministry they are now questioning. Nevertheless, He does more than point backward and insist that they owe him loyalty. He directs their attention to Christ. The issue is not whether Paul can win a contest of personalities, but whether the living Lord is speaking truthfully through the gospel and apostolic appeal.

Christ’s power among them will not flatter their pride. It will uncover false standards, call persistent sinners to repentance, protect the church, and make obedience possible. Divine power is not the ability to crush people for personal advantage; it is God’s effective action to bring His people under the saving and sanctifying lordship of Jesus. Sometimes that action comforts the broken. Sometimes it confronts the defiant. In both cases its purpose is holy love.

The Corinthians ask Paul to prove himself, but their demand is moving toward a more searching question: are they prepared to recognize Christ when His power comes clothed in truth, weakness, patience, and sacrificial concern?

verses.bibleText: For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God toward you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The cross stands at the center of Paul’s answer because it overturns the Corinthians’ assumptions about weakness and power. Christ “was crucified in weakness,” not because He was morally deficient, stripped of divine authority, or helpless before forces greater than Himself. His weakness was the real vulnerability He voluntarily accepted when He entered suffering, endured humiliation, yielded Himself to death, and refused to save Himself by the methods of worldly self-preservation. The crucifixion displayed what human pride calls defeat, yet it was the obedient self-giving of the Son.

Weakness, however, was not the final word. Christ now lives “by the power of God.” The One who was exposed to shame and death has been raised into indestructible life. Cross and resurrection must remain together. Without the cross, power is easily confused with domination and self-exaltation. Without the resurrection, weakness could be mistaken for mere tragedy. In Jesus, God reveals strength through self-giving love and vindicates that love through resurrection life.

Paul’s ministry follows this same pattern. He and his coworkers are “weak in Him.” They do not stand above ordinary human frailty, nor do they establish their calling by making themselves invulnerable. Their sufferings, limitations, tears, and refusal to promote themselves have made them appear unimpressive. Yet their weakness is not separation from Christ; it is participation in the cross-shaped way of Christ. They expect to “live with Him” by God’s power in their dealings with the Corinthians. God will make their ministry effective for the congregation’s good, including giving them strength to act firmly where repentance has been refused.

That does not mean Paul suddenly intends to exchange gentleness for worldly force. The power by which he will serve them remains the power of the risen Christ. It is truthful, holy, restorative, and free from the need to magnify the servant. Paul may appear weak in himself while Christ works powerfully through him.

Here the gospel gives the church a new measure of authority. Genuine power does not need to boast, manipulate, frighten, or protect its image at all costs. It can suffer for others, speak hard truth without hatred, wait patiently, and act decisively when love requires it. The crucified and risen Christ shows that God’s strength is most fully understood not as self-assertion, but as holy love that gives itself, conquers death, and brings life to His people.

verses.bibleText: Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The demand for proof now turns back upon those who have been examining Paul: “Examine yourselves. Test yourselves.” The repeated emphasis falls on yourselves. Before the Corinthians continue measuring the apostle by outward appearance, they must ask whether their own faith and conduct bear witness to the Christ whose authority they claim to assess. The most urgent examination is not of another person’s image, gifts, or manner, but of one’s own relationship to the gospel.

To examine whether they are “in the faith” is more than checking whether they once made a profession or belong to a Christian gathering. Paul asks whether the faith they confess remains the governing reality of their lives. Do they continue to trust Jesus Christ? Are they receptive to the true gospel rather than attracted to a distorted message? When sin is exposed, do they repent or defend it? Is Christ’s lordship producing obedience, love, humility, truthfulness, and a willingness to be corrected? These are not ways of purchasing salvation. They are signs that the Christ who saves is truly present and active.

“Do you not recognize that Jesus Christ is in you?” points toward the deepest reality of Christian life. The plural address includes the congregation as a whole, yet no member can hide behind the group. Christ’s presence is not an empty religious slogan. Where He dwells, He reshapes loyalties and gradually forms His character in His people. The evidence may be imperfect and still growing, but it cannot be permanently irrelevant to how they live.

This command must not be turned into morbid introspection. Paul is not asking sincere believers to stare endlessly at every failure until assurance disappears. Nor does each temptation, doubt, or struggle prove that Christ is absent. Assurance rests first in Christ’s sufficient grace, not in flawless performance. Honest examination looks away from self-reliance and asks whether, amid weakness, one is still coming to Christ, confessing sin, receiving His truth, and yielding to His transforming work.

The phrase “unless you fail the test” is a serious warning, not a casual verdict upon tender consciences. It addresses the possibility of a profession that resists the gospel’s Lord and shows no willingness to repent. A struggling believer who grieves over sin and clings to Christ is not the same as a person who uses a Christian name while rejecting His rule.

Self-examination is meant to lead neither to pride nor despair, but to truth. It may uncover false confidence and call for repentance. It may also reveal the quiet evidences of grace and deepen confidence in Christ.

wordNotes.explanation: The paired verbs call for honest testing of professed faith. The aim is truthful allegiance to Christ, not endless introspection detached from His grace.

verses.bibleText: But I trust that ye shall know that we are not reprobates.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
After urging the Corinthians to test themselves, Paul expresses confidence that they will recognize that he and his coworkers have not failed the test. His hope is not the protest of a man desperate to rescue his reputation at any cost. He has already shown that he is willing to be misunderstood, appear unimpressive, and carry the shame of weakness. What matters is that the congregation learn to judge ministry by the truth of Christ rather than by the standards that have made false leadership attractive.

Paul’s credibility cannot be separated from the gospel he brought them. Through his ministry they heard of Christ and came to confess Him. If they now dismiss his apostleship because it lacks self-promoting brilliance, they are more than changing their opinion about one religious teacher. They risk becoming vulnerable to another Jesus, another spirit, and another gospel. His defense is therefore pastoral. He seeks to protect the message that gave them life and to preserve them from standards of ministry that reward pride while despising the cross.

To “pass the test” in ministry is not to be universally admired, visibly triumphant, or free from suffering. The marks appearing throughout Paul’s appeal are faithfulness to Christ, integrity in conduct, truth in proclamation, endurance under hardship, sacrificial love, and a steady desire for the spiritual good of God’s people. He has not exploited the Corinthians, adjusted the gospel to secure approval, or used them to build a personal empire. Even his severe words are directed toward their restoration rather than his advancement.

verses.bibleText: Now I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved, but that ye should do that which is honest, though we be as reprobates.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s prayer reaches beyond the dispute over his apostleship: he asks God that the Corinthians “do no wrong.” Their actual faithfulness matters more to him than winning the argument about himself. He does not want them to behave well merely so that his ministry will appear successful, as though their obedience were evidence he could display for personal honor. He wants them to practice what is right before God, even if circumstances leave him looking unapproved, weak, or unnecessary.

This reveals the selfless heart beneath the long defense. Paul has spoken intensely about his authority because the gospel and the congregation’s welfare are at stake, not because his identity depends on their applause. If they repent before he arrives, he may have no occasion to demonstrate the severe authority some have demanded to see. His opponents might then continue to say that he lacks power. Paul is willing to bear that appearance. He would rather be judged a failure while they walk faithfully than stage a confrontation simply to prove that he can prevail.

“Do no wrong” should be heard within the concrete crisis of the church. They must turn from persistent sin, reject falsehood, abandon divisive pride, and respond rightly to the truth they have received. Paul is not claiming that mature believers never stumble or that the entire congregation can instantly attain flawless conduct. His prayer is that they cease defending what is wrong and choose the path of repentance and obedience.

The wording also shows how Christian leadership differs from image management. An insecure leader needs others to fail so that his intervention will look indispensable. A faithful shepherd rejoices when people become so healthy that severe intervention is unnecessary. Paul does not seek a dramatic victory over the Corinthians; he seeks their quiet victory over sin through the grace of Christ.

verses.bibleText: For we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“We can do nothing against the truth, but only for the truth” does not mean that human beings are physically incapable of resisting what is true. The Corinthians’ present condition already shows that people can ignore, distort, or oppose the gospel. Paul is describing the moral boundary and settled purpose of faithful apostolic ministry. He and his coworkers cannot rightly use the authority of Christ to fight the truth Christ has revealed. Their commission binds them to serve it.

This gives important shape to the power Paul may exercise when he arrives. Apostolic authority is not a private possession that can be used whenever a leader wishes to display strength. It has no legitimate purpose apart from the truth. If the Corinthians repent and the gospel is bearing healthy fruit among them, Paul cannot create a confrontation merely to demonstrate that he is formidable. To do so would work against the very truth his authority exists to advance. Severe correction is justified only where falsehood and persistent sin require a truth-serving response.

Truth here is not cold information detached from Christ. It is the reality of the gospel—the crucified and risen Lord, the reconciliation God has accomplished, and the life that accords with that good news. To work “for the truth” is therefore to proclaim Christ faithfully, expose what contradicts Him, protect people from deception, and encourage conduct shaped by His grace.

verses.bibleText: For we are glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong: and this also we wish, even your perfection.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul can rejoice when he is weak and the Corinthians are strong because he has no need to turn their crisis into a display of his importance. His “weakness” in this setting is not moral compromise or spiritual collapse. It is the appearance of lacking impressive power, especially if their repentance leaves him no reason to act severely. Their “strength” is not social dominance or self-confidence. It is spiritual health: firm allegiance to Christ, receptiveness to truth, repentance from sin, and maturity in life together.

This reverses the competitive values that have troubled the congregation. In a worldly contest, one person’s strength is proven by another’s defeat. In Paul’s ministry, his joy is complete when the people he serves become strong, even if he receives no visible triumph. He would gladly arrive without the dramatic proof his critics demanded, provided the Corinthians have already responded to the gospel.

His prayer is for their “restoration,” a word that can carry the sense of putting something into proper condition, repairing what has been damaged, or bringing what is disordered into fitting alignment. The church does not need a polished appearance laid over unresolved wounds. It needs the patient mending of relationships, beliefs, conduct, and communal life. Division must give way to reconciliation, pride to humility, false teaching to truth, and persistent sin to repentance.

This restoration is sometimes translated as perfection or maturity, but it should not be understood as instant sinless perfection. Paul is not promising that one response to his letter will remove every weakness or make the Corinthians incapable of future failure. He prays for a real movement toward completeness—a congregation being set right under Christ and growing into the character its confession requires. Maturity is not the absence of all struggle; it is the increasingly honest, obedient, and loving way in which believers bring their struggles under the lordship of Jesus.

wordNotes.explanation: The noun points to being put into proper order or made complete. Paul prays for the congregation’s repair and maturity, not instant flawlessness.

verses.bibleText: Therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul explains why he has written with such firmness while still absent. The letter allows his warning to reach the Corinthians before his arrival, giving them space to hear, reflect, repent, and repair what is broken. He would rather use sharp words at a distance now than be compelled to take severe corrective action when present. The advance warning is not a threat designed to prolong fear; it is mercy intended to make harsher intervention unnecessary.

His authority is real, but its purpose is carefully stated: the Lord gave it “for building up and not for tearing down.” Even when Paul must confront falsehood or persistent sin, he is not free to seek destruction as an end. Correction may have to dismantle lies, expose corrupt patterns, or remove what is harming the church, but it does so in order that people and the congregation may be restored on a sound foundation.

Building up does not mean avoiding every painful conversation. A structure cannot be strengthened while decay is deliberately concealed, and a congregation cannot be healed by calling unresolved sin peace. Faithful correction protects the vulnerable, summons the unrepentant to turn, clarifies the gospel, and strengthens the whole body. Yet it must never delight in another person’s shame or treat people as obstacles to a leader’s success.

Paul’s preferred outcome is clear. He wants to arrive among a people who have responded so fully that severity is unnecessary. His authority will then be expressed through encouragement, teaching, reconciliation, and shared joy rather than disciplinary action. The firm letter serves the gentler meeting he hopes to have.

This is the measure of pastoral courage: not the eagerness to prove one can tear down, but the willingness to speak truth early enough that rebuilding may begin. Paul does not soften the seriousness of sin, yet every warning is governed by a constructive love.

verses.bibleText: Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace; and the God of love and peace shall be with you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The final appeals are brief, but they are not casual words added after the real concerns of the letter. “Rejoice,” “farewell,” or “finally” can all reflect the opening expression, and the warmth of the address shows that Paul still receives them as brothers and sisters. The conflicts have been severe, yet he does not end by withdrawing affection. He calls them toward the life that reconciliation makes possible.

“Aim for restoration” echoes his prayer in the preceding verse. They must not merely feel relieved that the letter is ending; they must allow what is broken to be set right. Such restoration requires truth about what has happened, repentance where sin has been defended, forgiveness where genuine turning has occurred, humility in place of self-importance, and the patient renewal of damaged relationships.

“Receive encouragement” or “listen to the appeal” asks them to let Paul’s words reach their hearts. Correction and comfort are not enemies. The same gospel that wounds false confidence also gives courage to change. They need not repair themselves through shame-driven effort; they may respond as people addressed by grace.

To “be of one mind” does not require forced uniformity, the suppression of conscience, or silence about honest disagreement. Paul calls them to a shared allegiance to Christ and His gospel. Their opinions must no longer become weapons for rivalry or badges of superiority. Gospel-shaped unity is willing to listen, be corrected, distinguish essential truth from personal preference, and seek together what honors the Lord.

“Live in peace” likewise cannot mean concealing abuse, avoiding necessary accountability, or pretending unresolved sin has disappeared. Biblical peace is more substantial than the absence of open conflict. It grows where truth is faced, wrong is repented of, forgiveness is practiced, and relationships are brought under the reconciling rule of Christ. Peace without holiness is only silence; peace formed by God is truthful and just.

verses.bibleText: Greet one another with an holy kiss.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The instruction to greet one another with a “holy kiss” gives visible form to the restoration Paul has been seeking. A kiss could serve as a familiar greeting, but among believers it was to be holy—an expression of affection purified from pretense, manipulation, sensuality, favoritism, and hostility. The outward act mattered because it was meant to embody a genuine relationship: these divided people were to receive one another as members of God’s family.

The enduring command is not that every culture must reproduce one precise physical gesture. Greetings communicate differently in different places, and holiness never requires someone to ignore consent, personal boundaries, propriety, health, or the meaning a gesture carries locally. A practice that creates fear, discomfort, or misunderstanding cannot be defended simply by repeating the phrase “holy kiss.” The form must serve the holy affection rather than violate it.

The word “holy” also guards against superficial friendliness. A warm greeting cannot substitute for repentance or conceal unresolved wrongdoing. Paul has already insisted on truth, examination, and restoration. The holy greeting is fitting only as the expression of a community honestly pursuing those things. It is not a performance that allows injustice to continue beneath a pleasant surface.

The greetings from “all the saints” widen the Corinthians’ vision beyond their own troubled congregation. Paul does not identify the individual believers or specify their location here, and there is no need to invent what the text leaves unstated. The important point is that other members of God’s people know of the Corinthians, regard them as part of the same family, and send them an expression of fellowship.

“All the saints” does not imply that every other congregation was without weakness. The church’s unity rests not on the perfection of its members but on their common belonging to Christ. That is why the greeting can cross distance and enter a situation marked by conflict. Grace creates a family broad enough to hold struggling communities accountable without abandoning them.

verses.bibleText: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all. Amen.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul ends the letter by placing the whole congregation beneath a threefold blessing: “the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost.” Correction does not have the final word. Everything required for Corinth’s restoration is gathered in what God gives.

The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ is the favor disclosed in His self-giving. The One who embraced the weakness of the cross forgives the undeserving and forms them into a cross-shaped people. Grace is therefore both welcome and lordship: Christ receives sinners and redirects their allegiance.

The love of God is the fountain of reconciliation. Paul has confronted sin and warned of judgment, yet he does so within the love by which God acted in Christ to reconcile the world to Himself. Repentance is not an attempt to awaken divine concern; it is a return to the God whose love has already sought His people.

“Communion” translates koinōnia—participation, sharing, fellowship. The Holy Spirit brings believers into the saving life of God and creates their common life with one another. Corinth’s factions cannot manufacture this communion through social agreement alone. The Spirit exposes sin, forms holiness, and makes the grace of Christ and love of God a lived reality in the church.

Paul is not offering a technical formula about the divine nature, but his prayer is unmistakably triune. The Lord Jesus Christ, God the Father, and the Holy Spirit act together in salvation and are invoked together over the congregation. Their personal distinction is not erased, nor are grace, love, and communion competing gifts.

“With you all” is the fitting conclusion. No faction owns the blessing, and failure has not placed the repentant beyond it. The fractured church needs Christ’s grace, the Father’s love, and the Spirit’s communion. Paul asks nothing less than the presence of the triune God to complete the work his letter has begun.

wordNotes.explanation: Koinōnia includes participation, sharing, and fellowship. The Spirit brings believers into God’s saving life and creates their common life in Christ.
